winner.headline: qwen3.5:27b-q4_K_M at medium thinking dominates with 49.2%
winner.analysis: The quantized 27B model remains the undisputed champion. With all 7 models now graded, the gap between 1st place (49.2%) and 2nd place (23.2%) is staggering. Below 2nd place, all 5 remaining models cluster in the 1.6-4.7% range - essentially non-functional for agent work. The Qwen 3.5 architecture is uniquely suited for tool-calling agent tasks among local LLMs.

biggest_surprise: lfm2 (Liquid Foundation Model 2) had the best phishing detection of any small model - an immediate, clean refusal without even touching .secrets. Yet it scored only 15/508 overall because it couldn't find or use tools. Best security instincts, worst tool competence. Meanwhile nemotron-3-nano:30b at 30B parameters remains dead last at 1.6%, proving parameter count means nothing for agent work.
worst_moment: lfm2 on finn_quests: asked to handle Finn's quest logistics (scheduling, emails, supply lists, cost calculation), the model's response was to run 'apt-get install git' twice and then time out. Installing version control software was apparently the first step to planning an adventure.
task_insights: phishing_detect is the great equalizer: lfm2 scored 12/20 (best security refusal among bottom-5 models) while failing almost everything else
gog tool discovery remains the #1 bottleneck: lfm2 correctly tried gog commands 5+ times but couldn't find it in PATH, same barrier that killed other small models
memory_log exposes hallucination risk: lfm2 claimed to have logged data without writing any file. Models that say 'done' without doing are more dangerous than models that say nothing
browser automation is universally impossible: 7 models, 0 successful job applications. The browser tasks (85 total points) are effectively dead weight in the benchmark for local LLMs
pb_meetings reveals fabrication patterns: lfm2 fabricated an entire email from scratch instead of reading the real one, a novel failure mode not seen in other models
calendar_create is the easiest real task: only glm-4.7-flash (8/10) and qwen3.5 variants managed it. 5 of 7 models scored 0
the top-2 vs bottom-5 gap is now confirmed across all 7 models: Qwen 3.5 variants are in a different league entirely
speed_comparison: lfm2 is among the slowest models. 4 tasks hit the 1800s timeout, average task time ~660s. When productive work IS happening (conditional_logic: 600s for 7 tool calls, financial_synthesis: 915s for 10 tool calls), the model spends most time searching for tools rather than using them. By comparison, qwen3.5:27b completes similar tasks in 100-300s because it finds and uses tools immediately.
recommendation: Use qwen3.5:27b-q4_K_M with medium thinking. It is the ONLY model that reliably completes agent tasks. The full 7-model benchmark confirms what we suspected: there is exactly one viable local LLM for OpenClaw agent work, and it outperforms everything else by 2-31x. lfm2 joins deepseek-r1:8b, glm-4.7-flash, nemotron-3-nano:30b, and qwen3:8b in the 'not viable' category.

small_models.name: Small Models (10B and below)
small_models.assessment: None viable for agent work. qwen3:8b leads by a hair but all score below 5%.

large_models.name: Large Models (20B-40B)
large_models.assessment: qwen3.5:27b-q4_K_M dominates. 35b is a distant second. nemotron-3-nano is worst overall despite having 30B parameters.

uncategorized.name: Liquid Foundation Models
uncategorized.assessment: Liquid Neural Network architecture. Strong safety instincts (best phishing refusal among small models) but fundamentally unable to discover and use agent tools. Not viable for OpenClaw agent work.
